coredata.dc:title: The ligand effect of graphene quantum dots in NiFeO x /FeNi3 heterostructure for enhanced electrocatalytic valorization of poly(ethylene terephthalate) plastics
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. and Science Press. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. and Science Press. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Bo
Qian, Sheng
Ma, Shuhui
Zhang, Yi
Xue, Huaiguo
Jiang, Tengfei
Tian, Jingqi
coredata.dc:description:
Electrocatalytic valorization of disused poly (ethylene terephthalate) (PET) plastics into value-added chemicals emerges as a potential approach to address plastic pollution and resources upgrading, but it faces challenges in the development of efficient catalysts for PET-derived ethylene glycol (EG) electrooxidation. Herein, we proposed pyramid arrays on sheet Fe-doped NiO/FeNi3 (SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3) heterostructure, which is derived from the pyrolysis of MOF-on-MOF heterostructure growth triggered by graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Such SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 exhibits superior catalytic performance on the electrooxidation of EG (EGOR) from PET hydrolysate, with a formic acid (FA) selectivity of 91.5% and a Faradaic efficiency of 92%. The ligand effect of GQDs in both the catalyst design and improved electrocatalytic performance was studied with combined spectroscopy analysis and theoretical calculations, which revealed that such spatially separated NiFeO x and FeNi3 components by GQDs possess more active sites to anticipate in electrocatalytic EGOR, and the large sp 2 domains in GQDs possess a strong electron-withdrawing ability to reduce the electron density of bonded Ni and Fe, resulting in high-valenced Ni δ
+/Fe δ
+ in FeNi3 and Ni(2+
δ
) in NiO, respectively. Furthermore, the coordination number of Ni and Fe centers was lowered due to the steric effect of GQDs. Therefore, the adsorption of EG on Ni δ
+ for cascade dehydrogenation and C–C bond cleavage led to adsorbed FA that transferred to adjacent Fe for desorption, which was promoted by the enrichment of OH− on nearby Ni(2+
δ
) sites, along with optimized Gibbs free energy change in the multistep reaction pathway. This work provides an efficient multi-active-site catalyst for disused PET plastics valorization, thereby presenting a new approach to enhance the efficiency of PET plastics valorization reactions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Poly(ethylene terephthalate) plastic recycling
Electrocatalysis
Gaphene quantum dot
Ligand effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of C-GQDs, Ni-MOF/MIL-53(Fe), and SPA-NiFeOx/FeNi3
Synthesis of carboxyl functionalized graphene quantum dots (C-GQDs)
Synthesis of symmetrical pyramid arrays on sheet Ni-MOF/MIL-53(Fe)
Synthesis of symmetrical pyramid arrays on sheet NiFeOx/FeNi3 heterostructures (SPA-NiFeOx/FeNi3)
Electrochemical in-situ Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) reflectance and in-situ Raman spectroscopy
Electrocatalytic performance of SPA-NiFeOx/FeNi3 in ethylene glycol oxidation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3; (b) XRD patterns of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3, FeNi3, NiO, and C-GQDs; (c) low-magnification and (d) high-magnification SEM images; (e) HRTEM image with FFT pattern inset; (f) HAADF-STEM image and corresponding elemental mapping of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3.

(a) XPS survey spectra; (b) Ni 2p XPS spectra; (c) Fe 2p XPS spectra of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 and NiFeO x /FeNi3; (d and f) Ni K-edge and Fe K-edge XANES spectra of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3, NiFeO x /FeNi3, and reference Fe foil, Ni foil, and NiO; (e and g) Fourier-transformed (FT) k 3-weighted extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS) spectra of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 and NiFeO x /FeNi3; (h) Fe–O with BDC; (i) Fe–O with C-GQDs.

(a) LSV curves of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 and NiFeO x /FeNi3 at a scanning rate of 5 mV s−1 in 1 M KOH solution with and without 0.3 M PET hydrolysate; (b) Tafel slope curves of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 and NiFeO x /FeNi3 in 1 M KOH solution; (c) Nyquist diagram of OER in PET hydrolyzed products using SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 and NiFeO x /FeNi3 electrodes; (d) I-t curves in PET hydrolyzed products using SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 and NiFeO x /FeNi3 electrodes; (e) LSV curves of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 and NiFeO x /FeNi3 in 1 M KOH solution with 0.3 M PET hydrolysate normalized by ECSA. (f) Faraday efficiency and selectivity; (g) 1H NMR spectra of EGOR; (h) performance comparison radar chart; (i) operando Bode plots of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 during the OER and EGOR; (j) OCP of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 and NiFeO x /FeNi3 in 1 M KOH solution before and after adding EG; (k) LSV curves for the SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3||CoP electrolytic cell with and without the existence of 0.3 M PET hydrolysate in the anode cell.
(a) In-situ electrochemical FTIR spectra (different voltage); (b) in-situ electrochemical FTIR spectra (time lapse); (c) depiction of EG oxidation via C1 or C2 pathway.

(a) Optimized models of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 heterostructure and NiFeO x /FeNi3; (b) calculated adsorption energy of EG at the Ni or Fe site on (100) surface of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 heterostructure; (c) Bader charge diagram for SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 and NiFeO x /FeNi3; (d) EGOR free energy profile along C1 or C2 pathway over SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 heterostructure and NiFeO x /FeNi3; (e) electrostatic potential calculation; (f) schematic illustration of the EG oxidation mechanism over SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 heterostructure.

(a, b) SEM images, (c) XRD pattern, and (d) in-situ electrochemical Raman spectra of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 at different potentials; (e) Ni 2p, (f) Fe 2p, and (g) C 1s of post-SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 catalyst after the electrochemical operation.

head.ce:title: The ligand effect of graphene quantum dots in NiFeO x /FeNi3 heterostructure for enhanced electrocatalytic valorization of poly(ethylene terephthalate) plastics

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225002, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou 225002, Jiangsu, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots trigger the growth of pyramid arrays on sheet NiFeO x /FeNi3 heterostructures, which exhibit superior Faradic efficiency and selectivity of ethylene glycol electrooxidation toward efficient valorization of poly(ethylene terephthalate) plastics.

Electrocatalytic valorization of disused poly (ethylene terephthalate) (PET) plastics into value-added chemicals emerges as a potential approach to address plastic pollution and resources upgrading, but it faces challenges in the development of efficient catalysts for PET-derived ethylene glycol (EG) electrooxidation. Herein, we proposed pyramid arrays on sheet Fe-doped NiO/FeNi3 (SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3) heterostructure, which is derived from the pyrolysis of MOF-on-MOF heterostructure growth triggered by graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Such SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 exhibits superior catalytic performance on the electrooxidation of EG (EGOR) from PET hydrolysate, with a formic acid (FA) selectivity of 91.5% and a Faradaic efficiency of 92%. The ligand effect of GQDs in both the catalyst design and improved electrocatalytic performance was studied with combined spectroscopy analysis and theoretical calculations, which revealed that such spatially separated NiFeO x and FeNi3 components by GQDs possess more active sites to anticipate in electrocatalytic EGOR, and the large sp 2 domains in GQDs possess a strong electron-withdrawing ability to reduce the electron density of bonded Ni and Fe, resulting in high-valenced Ni δ
+/Fe δ
+ in FeNi3 and Ni(2+
δ
) in NiO, respectively. Furthermore, the coordination number of Ni and Fe centers was lowered due to the steric effect of GQDs. Therefore, the adsorption of EG on Ni δ
+ for cascade dehydrogenation and C–C bond cleavage led to adsorbed FA that transferred to adjacent Fe for desorption, which was promoted by the enrichment of OH− on nearby Ni(2+
δ
) sites, along with optimized Gibbs free energy change in the multistep reaction pathway. This work provides an efficient multi-active-site catalyst for disused PET plastics valorization, thereby presenting a new approach to enhance the efficiency of PET plastics valorization reactions.

ce:section.ce:para: Plastic waste causes severe pollution of the environment and ecosystem [1–3]. It is highly demanded to develop efficient technologies for end-of-life treatment of plastics. Polyethylene terephthalate (PET) serves as a synthetic polymer material such as packaging and textiles, which has been the most widely used plastic [4,5]. The recycling of PET mainly includes mechanical, chemical, and biological routes, among which the mechanical recycling of PET works on a small fraction (<20%) with inferior mechanical properties of the products, and the biological reforming routes encounter a low depolymerization rate [6–9]. Chemical reforming of PET involves the alkaline hydrolysis of PET into terephthalic acid (TPA) and ethylene glycol (EG) monomers, followed by the physical separation of TPA and the chemical transformation of EG into value-added chemicals [10]. Up to now, such chemical reforming of PET can be realized through thermal catalysis, photocatalysis, or electrocatalysis [11–13]. Electrocatalysis powered by sustainable electricity possesses great potential in PET reforming to catalyze the oxidation reaction of EG into value-added oxygenates such as formate and glycolate, with superior efficiency and selectivity to other chemical reforming routes [14]. However, there is a great demand for developing efficient electrocatalysts for wasted PET reforming.

There are multi-steps involved in the cascade electrooxidation of EG, such as the adsorption and activation of EG molecules, the continuous dehydrogenation of hydroxymethyl of EG to 2-hydroxyacetyl intermediates, and subsequent C–C cleavage or C=O activation to obtain C1 or C2 product. Designing multiple active sites that operate at each step to synergistically accomplish the whole catalytic reaction holds great promise in manipulating the adsorption, activation, and desorption behavior of the reactant, intermediate, and products [15]. Thereby, hetero-structured or multi-component catalysts have been developed, such as Pd-Ni(OH)2, Pd-CuCo2O4, NiO/FeNi3, and Pt-Ni(OH)2/NF [15–18], demonstrating the efficiency and feasibility of multiple active sites for EG electrooxidation. Up to now, the cost-effective and non-precious Ni-based materials have been regarded as one of the most efficient electrocatalysts for alcohols (methanol, ethylene glycol, glycerol, etc.) [19] oxidation owing to the capability to endow the reversible redox transformation between Ni(II) and Ni(III) that induces the oxidation of alcohols at relatively low potentials [20]. In such Ni-based catalysts, high-valenced Ni centers provide reactive oxygen species, especially OH* from OH− or H2O that anticipate the continuous dehydrogenation and C–C cleavage processes to achieve high activity [16,21]. Additionally, the coordination environment of metal centers can affect the formation of intermediates and the structure of the transition state, thereby regulating reaction pathways to influence selectivity [18,22]. Organic ligands that coordinate with the metal centers have been widely developed to modify the coordination environment of metal centers, ranging from large aromatic porphyrins [23], cyclic phosphine ligands [24], to small alkyl chain carboxylic acids [18,25]. The coordinative bonding between ligand and metal centers not only modifies the electron distribution around metal centers but also affects the spatial configuration of the metal center which is relative to intermediate adsorption. Therefore, precise design of Ni-based multi-component catalysts with high-valenced Ni sites and optimal coordination environment to sustain electrocatalytic activity and selectivity is highly demanded but very challenging.

Herein, we report the construction of symmetrical pyramid arrays on sheet NiFeO x /FeNi3 (SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3) heterostructures that were derived from metal-organic frameworks on metal-organic frameworks (MOF-on-MOF) heterostructure based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-triggered synthesis. Such prepared SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 exhibits superior electrooxidation performance of EG derived from PET waste, with 92% Faradic conversion efficiency and up to 91.5% formate selectivity, outperforming its NiFeO x /FeNi3 counterpart. Combined experimental and theoretical results indicated that in the multiple-components catalysts, the electron-withdrawing ability of the enlarged sp 2 domains in GQDs induces the formation of high-valenced Ni/Fe centers, which operates at elemental steps to synergistically promote the reaction kinetics of EG oxidation into formic acid (FA). In-situ Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) measurements were further employed to pinpoint crucial reaction intermediates in the C1 pathway. This work offers profound insights into catalyst design for the high-value-added conversion of PET.

All chemicals used in this study are analytical grade and were used without further purification. Terephthalic acid (BDC), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol (C2H5OH), ethylene glycol ((CH2OH)2), nitric acid (HNO3), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), potassium hydroxide (KOH), carbon black, and poly(ethylene terephthalate) (PET) granules were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. Nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O) and iron nitrate hexahydrate (Fe(NO)3·9H2O) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Pte Ltd (Singapore). Ti mesh (50 mesh m−2) was purchased from Shengda Technology. The water used in the experiment was purified by the Millipore system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of C-GQDs, Ni-MOF/MIL-53(Fe), and SPA-NiFeOx/FeNi3
Synthesis of carboxyl functionalized graphene quantum dots (C-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of C-GQDs was reported in previous work [26]. Typically, carbon black (0.4 mg) is added into a mixed solution containing nitric acid (40 mL) and water (60 mL), and the dispersion was transferred to a round-bottom flask for condensation reflux reaction at 130 °C for 30 h. After subsequent centrifugation and evaporation, the obtained product was dialyzed in water and freeze-dried to obtain C-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of symmetrical pyramid arrays on sheet Ni-MOF/MIL-53(Fe)
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, BDC (0.1246 g), NiCl2·6H2O (0.1603 g), Fe(NO)3·9H2O (0.0303 g), and C-GQDs (4.6 mg) were dispersed in a mixed solution of DMF, (CH2OH)2, and deionized water (32 mL:2 mL:2 mL), and then transferred to polytetrafluoroethylene reactor with a piece of pre-cleaned titanium mesh (2 cm × 4 cm). After hydrothermally treated in a sealed autoclave at 120 °C for 6 h, the symmetrical pyramid arrays on sheet Ni-MOF/MIL-53(Fe) on Ti mesh were taken out and thoroughly rinsed with deionized water before drying. NiFe-MOF without the addition of C-GQDs was prepared through a similar method for comparison.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of symmetrical pyramid arrays on sheet NiFeOx/FeNi3 heterostructures (SPA-NiFeOx/FeNi3)
ce:section.ce:para: Such prepared SPA-MOFs on Ti mesh were placed in the tube furnace and annealed at 350 °C for 2 h with a ramping rate of 5 °C min−1. For comparison, NiFe-MOF supported on Ti mesh was also annealed at the same condition to obtain a NiFeO x /FeNi3 counterpart.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected on Bruker D8 Advance with Cu K α radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å). The morphology and structure of the samples were determined by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Zeiss_Supra55) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN operated at 300 kV). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on an ESCALAB 250Xi using Mg as the excitation source. X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) was measured at RapidXAFS 2M.

The electrochemical measurements were carried out using the CHI 660D Electrochemical analyzer (CH instrument) equipped with a conventional three-electrode system with KOH (1.0 M) as electrolyte. The SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3, saturated calomel electrode (SCE), and platinum wire were employed as working electrodes, reference electrodes, and counter electrodes, respectively. The potential refers to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) following the equation: E RHE = E SCE + 0.059pH + 0.242. All electrochemical data are displayed with the iR correction. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was recorded over the frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz. The polarization curve was acquired at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1, and the electrode area for all electrochemical tests was 0.5 cm × 0.5 cm. Tafel slope can be extracted from the linear portions of the corresponding Tafel plots, which is fitted to the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curve obtained with a sweep speed of 5 mV s−1 in 1.0 M KOH with 0.3 M EG. All electrochemical data are displayed with the iR correction. For the two-electrode system measurements, the SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 and a self-supported CoP [27] were used as anode and cathode, respectively, with an electrode area of 0.25 cm2. The polarization curves were obtained with a sweeping rate of 5 mV s−1 in 1.0 M KOH with or without 0.3 M EG, at a positive sweeping direction in the potential window.

The capacitive currents were measured in a potential window, where no faradic process is observed (0.0–0.1 V vs. RHE). The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves were collected at various sweep rates from 10 to 200 mV s−1. Electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) was estimated from the obtained double-layer capacitance (C dl), which can be calculated following the equations listed below.
(1)
C
d l
=
Q U
=
d Q / d t
d U / d t
=
j r
where Q is the quantity of electric charge per unit area, U is the voltage (V), j is the current density (mA cm−2 mg−1), and r is the scan rate (V s−1). As such, the C dl for SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 and NiFeO x /FeNi3 was calculated to be 8.65 and 6.98 mF cm−2, and the corresponding ECSA was calculated to be 216.25 and 174.50 cm2.

High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (LC-20A) detector is an ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (UV-VIS) detector for the analysis of electrooxidation reactants and products. Typically, the PET hydrolysate was prepared by the addition of PET particles (2.0 g) into 1.0 M KOH aqueous solution (50 mL) to form a suspension, which was further hydrothermally treated at 200 °C for 12 h and acidized with 0.5 M H2SO4 to remove terephthalic acid. Then the pH of the hydrolysate was adjusted to 14 for further use. The concentration of EG in the prepared hydrolysate is 0.3 M, which was determined by HPLC. Specifically, after potentiostatic electrolysis, 100 μL of the electrolytic solution was taken out and acidified to 1 mL with dilute sulfuric acid to remove para-phthalate. Then the remaining solution was analyzed by HPLC. The specific parameters are set as follows: the wavelength of the UV detector is set at 210 nm, the mobile phase is 5 mM of dilute sulfuric acid, and the flow rate is 0.6 mL min−1. The determination was performed in C18 Column (4.6 mm × 150 mm Shim-pack GWs 5 μm) with a duration of not more than 10 min each.

The formate selectivity efficiency was calculated according to Eq. (2), and the Faraday efficiency of glycol oxidation was calculated according to Eq. (3).
(2)
Y i e l d
o f
f o r m a t e =
2 × f o r m a t e
( m o l )
i n i t i a l
E G
( m o l )
× 100 %
(3)
F a r a d a i c
e f f i c i e n c y =
2 × f o r m a t e
( m o l )
× 96485
( C
m o l
- 1
)
t o t a l
p a s s e d
c h a r g e
( C )
× 100 %

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical in-situ Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) reflectance and in-situ Raman spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: Attenuated total reflection surface-enhanced infrared absorption spectroscopy (ATR-SEIRAS) was used for in-situ FTIR measurements on a Shimadzu IRXross FTIR spectrometer with silicon as the prismatic window at room temperature. Electrochemical in-situ FTIR experiments were studied in an alkaline solution (0.1 M KOH with/without 0.3 M EG) and the results were recorded at 0.1 V intervals from 1.4 to 1.8 V (vs. RHE). In-situ FTIR measurements were performed using a Mercury-Cadmium-Telluride (MCT) detector equipped with liquid nitrogen cooling. The measurement was completed in an alkaline solution (0.1 M KOH + 0.3 M EG) with a three-electrode system, in which the synthesized electrocatalyst, saturated calomel electrode, and platinum wire were used as the working electrodes, reference electrodes, and counter electrodes, respectively. FTIR measurements are recorded in a range of 1.4–1.8 V (vs. RHE) with an interval of 0.1 V. In-situ Raman spectroscopy was measured on a Raman microscope (LabRAM HR Evolution) equipped with a 7 mW Nd:YAG laser (633 nm) as the excitation source to record the Raman spectra in the range of 200–800 cm−1. Electrochemical in-situ Raman experiments were studied in a Raman electrochemical flow cell with a three-electrode setup with an electrolyte (1 M KOH + 0.3 M EG) using the amperometric i-t curve (IT) technique at various potentials from open-circuit potential (OCP) to 0.8 V.

All calculations were performed using Gaussian 09 software, with the b3lyp level of theory26 and a 6-311+g(2d, p) basis set [28]. During the structure optimization, a standard energy convergence criterion of 10−6 a.u. was used. The self-consistent field (SCF) energy convergence criterion was set as 10−8 a.u. The electrostatic potential (ESP) was mapped onto an iso-density surface with a density of 0.001 a.u. The Mulliken population analysis was used to estimate the partial atomic charges [29,30]. All calculations were carried out using the Gaussian 09 program package.

All the calculations were performed by using the Vienna ab initio Simulation Program (VASP) [31,32]. The projector-augmented wave (PAW) method is employed to describe the interaction between ions and electrons [33], while the exchange-correlation potential is obtained through the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) in Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) [34]. The plane-wave cutoff and a Mokhorst-Pack mesh of k-points are 450 eV and 3 × 3 × 1, respectively. A Gaussian smearing of 0.05 eV to the orbital occupation is applied during the geometry optimization and for the total energy computations, whilst a tetrahedron method with Blöchl corrections was employed for the accurate electronic structure calculations. The FeNi3 (100) surface with 4 × 4 supercell and NiFeO x (100) surface with 3 × 3 supercell, which have the smallest lattice mismatch of the interface, are employed to construct the SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 heterojunction structure. A vacuum layer of 20 Å is selected for interface models to cut the periodic interactions. The DFT-D3 semiempirical correction was described via Grimme's scheme method [35]. An effective strength parameter U eff = U − J of the Hubbard on-site Coulomb correlation was set at 3.40 eV and applied to the Ni 3d states to improve the treatment of electronic correlations in the DFT calculations [36]. The Gibbs free energy change (ΔG) for each elemental step was defined as [37].
(4)
Δ G = Δ E + Δ Z P E - T Δ S
where ΔE and ΔZPE were the adsorption energy based on density functional theory calculations and the zero-point energy correction respectively. ΔS was the changes in zero point entropy at 298.15 K, which was calculated from the vibrational frequencies.

The symmetrical pyramid arrays on sheet NiFeO x /FeNi3 (SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3) heterostructures were prepared by hydrothermal growth of NiFe-based MOF-on-MOF and subsequent annealing as depicted in Fig. 1 (a). Carboxyl-functionalized graphene quantum dots (C-GQDs) triggered the hetero-growth of symmetrical pyramid arrays of NiFe-MOF on Ni-MOF sheet (SPA-NiFe-MOF), which were characterized by SEM and TEM (Fig. S1a and b). XRD pattern confirms the diffraction peaks identified in Ni-MOF (Fig. S1c). It is noted that without the addition of C-GQDs, there was only sheet-like NiFe-MOF with characteristic peaks similar to those of Ni-MOF (Fig. S1d, e). C-GQDs possess a unique structure as ligands for the precursor MOF synthesis, which are featured with an enlarged sp 2 domain in the polyaromatic conjugation and carboxyl groups on the edge. GQDs compete with small BDC molecules to chelate with metal ions at the interface of the MOF heterostructure, which triggers the growth of the pyramid arrays on the sheet MOF heterostructure. Benefiting from the chemical and thermal stability of C-GQDs, the sp 2 carbon domain is preserved at the interface of NiFeO x and FeNi3 in the heterostructure. C-GQDs are synthesized via the acid exfoliation approach. Detailed characterizations are shown in Fig. S2. TEM images show the good dispersion of such synthesized C-GQDs with an average lateral size of 2.18 ± 0.05 nm (Fig. S2a), with an inter-planar distance of 0.21 nm ascribed to the (010) plane of graphene (Fig. S2b). FTIR spectrum suggests the presence of peaks at 1209, 1415, 1621, 1712, and 3436 cm−1, which correspond to the C–O, C–H, CC, –COO, and –OH bands, respectively (Fig. S2c), confirming the presence of oxygen-containing groups in C-GQDs. XPS survey spectrum indicates the presence of C and O elements in C-GQDs (Fig. S2d). Deconvolution of the C 1s spectrum shows peaks corresponding to C–C, C–O, and CO at 284.8, 286.2, and 288.5 eV, respectively (Fig. S2e). The O 1s spectrum shows peaks at 531.8, 533.2, and 534 eV attributed to CO, O–H, and C–O, respectively (Fig. S2f). All these analyses indicate the dominant presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in C-GQDs. The number of C-GQDs was varied to control the morphology and density of the secondary NiFe-MOF on sheets. When increasing the amount of C-GQDs, the size and thickness of the secondary NiFe-MOF firstly increase, and then the thickness decreases to form dense sheet arrays (Fig. S3). This morphology change could be attributed to that C-GQDs as ligands coordinate with Ni and Fe at the interface of MOF-on-MOF, which triggers the nucleation of NiFe-MOF on Ni-MOF sheet with orientated growth.

After annealing, the crystal structure of the product was analyzed by XRD (Fig. 1b). Diffraction peaks located at 44.2°, 51.5°, and 75.8° were identified to the (111), (200), and (220) planes in FeNi3 (JCPDS No. 38-0419), and diffraction peaks at 37.2°, 43.2°, and 62.8° can be ascribed to the (111), (200), and (220) planes in NiO (JCPDS No. 47-1049). The counterpart NiFeO x /FeNi3 that is synthesized without C-GQDs exhibits a similar XRD pattern to that of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 (Fig. S4), indicating that the addition of C-GQDs has negligible influence on the crystal structure of the product. SEM images confirm that the symmetrical pyramid arrays on the sheet configuration of the precursor MOF have been well preserved during annealing but with a rough surface (Fig. 1c and d). TEM images further reveal the pyramid arrays on the sheet configuration of the heterostructure (Fig. S5). High-resolution TEM image taken from the interface shows lattice fringes of 0.204, 0.241, and 0.221 nm, which correspond to the (111) plane in FeNi3, (111) plane in NiO, and (010) plane in GQDs, respectively, revealing that the sheets and pyramids consist of NiO and FeNi3 while C-GQDs are located around the interface in the heterostructure. The corresponding fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern presents two patterns that are characteristic of fcc-NiO and orthorhombic FeNi3 crystals (Fig. 1e), which is consistent with XRD results. High-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) images coupled with element mapping images were further collected to reveal the dispersion of each element. It is observed that Ni, C, N, and O elements are uniformly distributed on the entire heterostructure, while Fe is mainly concentrated on the pyramids, which manifests the formation of NiFeO x /FeNi3 heterostructure (Fig. 1f). Since the amount of C-GQDs in NiFe-MOFs leads to the formation of NiFeO x /FeNi3 heterostructures with different densities of secondary pyramids (Fig. S6), the effect of the content of carboxyl groups in C-GQDs on the heterostructure formation was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S7 and Table S1, the content of –COOH in C-GQDs decreases with a shorter acid exfoliation time (C-GQDs-24 h). Accordingly, C-GQDs-24 h leads to the sparse formation of secondary pyramids in SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 (Fig. S8), further confirming the ligand effect of C-GQDs at the interface of MOF-on-MOF to induce heterostructure growth.

The surface chemistry of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 was investigated using XPS. XPS survey spectra exhibit the peaks of Ni, Fe, C, and O in both SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 and NiFeO x /FeNi3 samples (Fig. 2 a). The deconvoluted C 1s spectra show sp 2 C–C, C–O–C, and C–O peak at 284.8, 286.7, and 288.9 eV [27,38], respectively (Fig. S9). From Table S2, the increased content of C–C in SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 compared with that in NiFeO x /FeNi3 might arise from the enlarged sp 2 domains in C-GQDs. As depicted in Ni 2p spectrum of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3, the presence of Ni(0) is evidenced by the two characteristic peaks at 855.7 and 873.3 eV, whereas the peaks at 857.2 and 875.3 eV [39,40] are attributed to Ni(II) 2p 3/2 and 2p 1/2, respectively (Fig. 2b), demonstrating the coexistence of metallic Ni and Ni2+. In the Fe 2p spectrum of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3, the peaks at 708.5, 711.8, and 715.6 eV could be associated with Fe(0), Fe(II), and Fe(III) in Fe 2p 3/2, and the peaks at 724.0, 727.3, and 731.5 eV [41,42] could be associated with Fe(0), Fe(II), Fe(III) in Fe 2p 1/2, respectively (Fig. 2c), which confirm the mixed valence of Fe. These results are indicative of the presence of FeNi3 alloys and oxides in the heterostructure. It is worthwhile to note that the Ni(0) and Ni(II) peaks and Fe(0) peaks in SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 positively shift compared to those in NiFeO x /FeNi3, indicating that C-GQDs possessing strong electron- withdrawing capability induce electron transfer from the metal centers to the conjugated π domains, resulting in positively-charged Ni δ
+/Fe δ
+ in FeNi3 and high-valenced Ni(2+
δ
) in NiFeO x .

In order to further investigate the coordination environments and electronic structures of Ni and Fe in SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 and NiFeO x /FeNi3, X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) of Ni and Fe K-edge was performed. In the XANES spectroscopy (Fig. 2f), the spectral lines of Fe in SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 and NiFeO x /FeNi3 are located around the position of the Fe foil while the spectral lines of Ni fall between Ni foil and NiO, which suggests the Fe with a valence state close to zero and Ni with δ+ (0 < δ < 2) in both samples. Specifically, the average oxidation value is calculated to be 0.1 and 0.16 for Ni and Fe in SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3, which is higher than that in NiFeO x /FeNi3 (Fig. S10). Both SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 and NiFeO x /FeNi3 exhibit a weak peak corresponding to Fe–O around 1.6 Å and Ni–O at 1.4 Å, respectively (Fig. 2e). Moreover, in the Fe and Ni spectral lines, SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 shows a dominant peak at 2.2 and 2.17 Å (Fig. 2g), respectively, which both are positively shifted compared with that in NiFeO x /FeNi3 (2.17 and 2.14 Å), indicating a higher valence state of Fe and Ni induced by GQDs, which is consistent with XPS analysis. The coordination number of both Fe and Ni decreased in SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 compared with that in NiFeO x /FeNi3 (Tables S3 and S4), which could be rationalized to the steric effect of the nano-sized GQDs as ligands compared with the small BDC molecules (Fig. 2h and i). These results would influence the adsorption and activation of reactants and intermediates toward optimized electrocatalytic reaction pathways. The maximum peak intensity of the Fe–Fe bond negatively shifted in SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 and NiFeO x /FeNi3 compared with that in Fe foil (7.3 and 7.2 Å−1 vs. 8.2 Å−1), and a similar behavior was observed in the case of Ni edges (Figs. S11 and S12).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic performance of SPA-NiFeOx/FeNi3 in ethylene glycol oxidation
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the electrochemical performance of EG oxidation reaction (EGOR) from PET hydrolysate of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3, a PET hydrolysate solution was first prepared by alkaline hydrolysis of commercial PET particles at 200 °C for 24 h in 1 M KOH. In a three-electrode cell with PET hydrolysate solution as the electrolyte, Fig. 3 (a) displays the EGOR polarization curves obtained from LSV measurements. SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 shows an oxidative peak centered at ∼1.45 V, which could be assigned to the reversible oxidation of Ni2+ to Ni3+ [43], with a larger peak current intensity than that of NiFeO x /FeNi3 which could be attributed to the higher content of Ni in the catalyst (Table S2). SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 shows an earlier start for EGOR than oxygen evolution reaction (OER) with larger current densities, which leads to the EGOR polarization curve completely masking the oxidation peak, suggesting its potential EGOR capability [44]. In detail, SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 requires overpotentials of 200 mV and 250 mV to achieve a current density of 100 mA cm−2 for EGOR and OER, respectively, both of which are superior to these of NiFeO x /FeNi3. SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 exhibits a lower Tafel slope (53 mV dec−1) compared to NiFeO x /FeNi3 for EGOR (Fig. 3b), which indicates fast EGOR kinetics. As shown in the Nyquist diagram (Fig. 3c), SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 obtains a lower transmission resistance of 3.509 Ω, which further manifests a fast EGOR kinetics. To study the stability of the catalyst, a potentiostatic electrolysis test was carried out for 100 h for both catalysts (Fig. 3d). It is found that SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 maintains its original electrocatalytic activity with 4.36% decay under current density of 100 mA cm−2, while the NiFeO x /FeNi3 loses a significant ca. 66.04% of its activity, demonstrating the long-term stability of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 heterostructure for EGOR. In order to investigate the potential of industrial applications, the stability of such SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 electrode at a current density of 300 mA cm−2 was evaluated (Fig. S13). It is found that the SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 electrode only exhibits a current density decay of 7.63% during the 100-h test, indicating the great potential for industrial application. The electrical C dl was obtained from a series of CV curves in the non-Faradic region (Fig. S14a, b). Accordingly, The ECSA of the catalysts was calculated, and SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 heterostructure delivers a larger ECSA value than NiFeO x /FeNi3 (216.25 vs. 174.5 cm2, in Fig. S14c). The current density is further normalized by ECSA to study the intrinsic activity of the catalysts (Fig. 3e), in which SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 heterostructure shows greater current density compared with NiFeO x /FeNi3, suggesting their higher intrinsic catalytic activity. The amount of C-GQDs and carboxyl group in SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 heterostructure was optimized (Figs. S15 and S16). It is seen that too many loadings of pyramids result in dense stacking which blocks mass transfer, while rare pyramids cannot provide sufficient active sites.

HPLC was employed to analyze the main products and by-products that may be produced in the oxidation process of EG. As shown in Figs. S17 and Fig. S18, the peaks of Glycolic acid (GA), FA, and EG appeared in 2.3, 2.7, and 3.5 min, respectively, according to the standard chemicals. Faradaic efficiency (FE) and selectivity are important parameters to evaluate the EGOR performance. To give quantitative analysis, a potentiostatic electrolysis operated at an optimized potential of 1.6 V for 120 min was conducted, which delivers the highest yield of FA, with the Faraday efficiency reaching 92% and a selectivity of 91.5% (Fig. 3f). As the voltage increases, the FA yield and FE gradually decrease. The cause may be the deep oxidation of FA into CO2 or water oxidation. The electrolytes before and after electrolysis were analyzed by 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy (Fig. 3g). 1H NMR spectra identified peaks corresponding to EG at 3.5 ppm, GA at 3.9 ppm, and FA at 8.3 ppm, respectively. Besides, 13C NMR measurements were conducted to verify the products. As shown in Fig. S19, no peaks corresponding to oxalate (a possible by-product) could be found, but the characteristic peaks of GA and FA were observed, which is consistent with the 1H NMR data. The total organic carbon (TOC) before and after EGOR was determined on a Shimadu TOC-L CPH machine, which exhibits similar carbon content for both samples (Fig. S20), excluding the mineralization of EG during electrocatalysis. The EGOR performance of such SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 was further summarized and compared with advanced non-noble-Ni-based catalysts regarding key parameters including overpotential, FE, selectivity, and stability, as shown in the radar diagram in Fig. 3(h) and Table S5. It is found that such SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 behaves outstandingly in both FE and selectivity, outperforming most non-noble-metal- based EGOR catalysts.

EIS was conducted to investigate the interfacial electrochemical behavior of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 during OER and EGOR. The Bode plots of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 reveal the characteristic peak centered at 0.1 Hz in the low-frequency region at the potential of 1.45 V for the OER. In comparison, the low-frequency characteristic peak appears at a relatively lower potential of 1.3 V for the EGOR and gradually shifts to the lower phase when elevating the potential, which could be ascribed to the occurrence of thermodynamically more favorable EGOR (Fig. 3i). As the first step in electrocatalytic EG conversion in alkaline electrolytes, the EG adsorption behavior on different catalysts in the Helmholtz inner layer is evaluated by OCP tests. The addition of 30 mM EG into the alkaline electrolyte induces an OCP value of 0.55 and 0.5 V on SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 and NiFeO x /FeNi3, respectively, indicating more favourated EG adsorption on SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 (Fig. 3j) [45]. The superior electrochemical performance of such designed SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 could be rationalized to the following aspects. Firstly, the pyramid arrays on sheet configuration introduce more active sites for electrocatalysis with larger ECSA to benefit the interaction with reactant EG molecules. Secondly, such vertically aligned pyramid arrays on the nanosheet geometry create channels that enhance the diffusion of molecules, allowing for better mass transfer and reactant accessibility, which is crucial for electrocatalytic reaction kinetics. Moreover, the vertical electron transfer pathway along these pyramids on the sheet also contributes to the fast electron transfer during electrocatalysis.

Regarding the excellent catalytic activity and selectivity of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 for the generation of FA from the oxidation of PET hydrolysate, a two-electrode system was assembled using SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 and CoP as anode and cathode (Fig. 3k), respectively. Such a two-electrode system only requires a driving voltage of 1.4 V to deliver a current density of 100 mA cm−2, which is comparable with most advanced electrolysis cells containing PET hydrolysate [46].

Electrochemical in-situ FTIR experiments were performed to identify the intermediates and products during the reaction pathway of EGOR. As shown in (Fig. 4 a and b), either elevating the oxidation potential or extending the reaction time leads to the consumption of EG with a decrease in the peak of adsorbed EG at 1338 cm−1. Meanwhile, the peak of the stretching vibration of 2-hydroxyacetyl (*OC–CH2OH) and an aldehyde belonging to glyoxal was observed at 1635 and 1087 cm−1, respectively, indicating that the cascade dehydrogenation process occurs and glyoxal is the key intermediate in EG oxidation over SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 electrocatalyst. The peak at 1573 cm−1 could be attributed to the symmetric COO stretching vibration of FA, demonstrating that the C–C cleavage occurs. At the same time, the glycolic band was also found at 1246 cm−1, which may be caused by the conversion of glycolic aldehyde to GA by Cannizzaro reaction in an alkaline solution. The continuous increase in the peak at 1635 cm−1 and steady signal of the peak at 1087 cm−1 evidence that FA serves as the main product following the C1 pathway while a small fraction of GA coexists in the system [47]. The corresponding pathways are depicted in Fig. 4(c).

To investigate the mechanism of C-GQDs improving the electrocatalytic performance of EG oxidation in SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 heterostructure, FT calculations were performed. The active sites for EGOR consist of high-valence Ni δ
+/Fe δ
+ in FeNi3 and Ni(2+
δ
) in NiFeO x , which were located at the interface between FeNi3 and NiFeO x that mainly presented in the secondary pyramids and primary nanosheets linked by C-GQDs, respectively. Based on the crystal structure and coordination environment characterizations, an optimized computational model for SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 heterostructure consisting of NiO and FeNi3 with the interfacial C-GQD coordinated with Fe atom on the (100) surface of FeNi3 and O on the (100) surface of NiO was established (Fig. 5 a). EG adsorption is the first step in the cascade dehydrogenation process. So the EG adsorption energy (E EG) on different metallic sites in the SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 heterostructure was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the value of E EG on Ni and Fe in FeNi3 is much lower than that on the Ni site in NiO (−2.21 and −1.96 eV vs. −1.64 eV), indicating that EG molecules are preferentially adsorbed on Ni sites in FeNi3. Bader charge analysis also confirms that the electronic state of Ni and Fe in FeNi3 is 0.44 e and 0.72 e, and that of Ni in NiO is 0.21 e in SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3, while the corresponding value was 0.34 e, 1.02 e, and 0.98 e in NiFeO x /FeNi3 (Fig. S21), which suggests that these metal centers lost more electrons through the positively charged O to C-GQDs, leading to the increased valence state of metal centers. Regarding the requirement of OH− participation in EG electrooxidation, either in terms of the formation of NiOOH (OH− + Ni(OH)2 − e− = NiOOH + H2O) or the dehydrogenation and C–C cleavage processes (Fig. 4c), the enrichment of OH− on the catalyst is essential. Therefore, an oxophilicity and high-valence Ni(2+
δ
) in NiO favors OH− enrichment around the catalyst, which can synergistically promote EG electrooxidation [16]. The adsorbed EG molecules were activated and the glyoxal intermediate was generated for C–C cleavage to form the adsorbed FA, which was transferred to adjacent Fe for desorption. These Ni δ
+/Fe δ
+ and Ni(2+
δ
) are located at the interfacial FeNi3 and NiO, respectively. The Gibbs free energy change (ΔG) was calculated in the cascade dehydrogenation steps and C–C cleavage involving the generation of CH2OHCH2OH*, CH2OHCH2O*, CH2OHCHO*, CH2OCHO*, CHOCHO*, and HCOOH* intermediates (Fig. 5d). It is observed that SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 heterostructure exhibited favorable kinetics to accomplish each step compared with NiFeO x /FeNi3, confirming that the presence of GQDs optimizes the ΔG along the multistep reaction pathways. To be specific, the first rate-determining step (RDS) for both samples is the first dehydrogenation step from CH2OHCH2OH* to CH2OHCH2O* with the reaction energy barrier of −0.65 and −0.80 eV for SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 and NiFeO x /FeNi3, respectively. Regarding the formed HCOOH* intermediate for desorption to generate FA, the ΔG on Ni and Fe sites in FeNi3 was calculated. As shown in Fig. S22, the Fe site exhibits a smaller ΔG value compared with the Ni site (0.58 vs. 0.79 eV), demonstrating the favorable transfer of formed HCOOH* intermediate from the Ni site to the adjacent Fe site for desorption. Such a lower barrier to overcome SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 compared with the counterpart indicates a higher catalytic activity. To summarize, the incorporation of C-GQDs enables the optimization of the energy barrier of the reactions.

It is inevitable to generate GA as a by-product in this electrochemical system. In alkaline media, FA and GA coexisted in the form of carboxylate cations, both of which coordinate with positive-charged Fe centers through the deprotonation of carboxyl groups. Thus the atomic charges (Q O) of the negatively charged O atom are put forward as an important parameter for evaluation of the coordination capability with metal centers. It is obvious that GA possesses more electrons on this O atom and thus the carboxyl (Fig. 5e), indicating its higher bonding strength with positively charged Fe centers which might lead to the difficulty to desorption and thus inhibits the Cannizzaro reaction (Fig. 5f).

The ligand effect of C-GQDs in enhancing the catalytic performance has been demonstrated both experimentally and theoretically. Firstly, the unique pyramid arrays on sheet configuration of SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 introduce more active sites for electrocatalysis, and the vertically aligned pyramid arrays on the nanosheet geometry create a vertical electron transfer pathway which contributes to the fast electron transfer during electrocatalysis. Secondly, combined spectroscopic analysis with theoretical results indicated the enlarged sp 2 domains in GQDs induce the formation of high-valenced Ni/Fe centers with lower coordination numbers of both Ni and Fe, owing to the larger π-conjugation endowed with strong electron-withdrawing ability, which promotes the cascade dehydrogenation and C–C bond cleavage, leading to optimized Gibbs free energy change in the multistep reaction pathway of EGOR. Thirdly, SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 possesses multiple active sites, among which the adsorbed EG molecules were activated on Ni δ
+ for cascade dehydrogenation and C–C bond cleavage to form adsorbed FA and transferred to adjacent Fe for desorption, which was promoted by the enrichment of OH− on nearby Ni(2+
δ
) sites, to perform a synergistic promotion mechanism. Compared with the traditional elemental doping strategies for improved EGOR either in terms of single cation/anion doping or cation-anion dual doping [48], most of which aim to regulate the electronic structure of the catalyst that could influence the adsorption/desorption behavior of the reactant, intermediate, and product during EGOR [49,50], this GQD-doping strategy not only modulates the electronic structure of metallic centers but also manipulates the morphology of the catalyst to synergistically improve the EGOR performance.

Detailed characterizations of the catalyst after the electrochemical experiments were performed by SEM, XRD, and XPS (Fig. 6 ). SEM images show the good preservation of morphology after long-term electrocatalysis for 100 h, with intact pyramid arrays on sheet configuration but increased surface roughness (Fig. 6a, b). Fig. 6(c) exhibits the XRD pattern of the post-catalyst, with diffraction peaks located at 18.4°, 37.3°, and 66.7° that correspond to the crystal NiOOH (JCPDS No. 06-0141) [51] and peaks located at 44.3°, 51.5°, and 75.9° that are assigned to the similar FeNi3 phase in Fig. 1(b), confirming the generation of NiOOH with preserved FeNi3 in SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 during EGOR. To track down the phase transformation during electrocatalysis, in-situ electrochemical Raman measurements were employed (Fig. 6d). At low potentials, two Raman peaks at 447 and 490 cm−1 which correspond to A1g stretching modes of NiII–OH and NiII–O in NiO were identified [52]. When the applied potentials were higher than 0.6 V, two peaks appeared at 476 and 555 cm−1, which correspond to the Eg bending and A1g stretching vibrations of NiIII–O in NiOOH [14], confirming the formation of NiOOH during electrocatalysis. A further increase in applied potential leads to the rise in the doublet peaks at 475 and 555 cm−1 and a gradual degradation of the stretching peak at 700 cm−1, indicating that the formed NiOOH accounts for the EGOR activities [53]. XPS analysis was used to investigate the elemental composition and surface electronic states of the post-catalyst. XPS survey spectrum (Fig. S23) demonstrates the presence of Ni, Fe, C, and O in the post-SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 catalyst. As depicted in Fig. 6(e and f), the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Ni in post-SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 consisted of deconvoluted peaks of Ni0, Ni2+, Ni3+, and satellite peaks in both Ni 2p 3/2 and Ni 2p 1/2, and Fe 2p spectrum shows the presence of Fe0, Fe2+, Fe3+, and satellite peak, respectively. It is observed that Ni 2p and Fe 2p peaks in post-SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 exhibit positive shifts and the ratio of Ni3+/Ni2+ increases from 0 to 0.58 (Table S6), which could be attributed to newly formed crystal NiOOH. The ratio of Fe3+/Fe2+ exhibits a slight change after long-term electrochemical operation, which could be attributed to the surface oxidization. C 1s spectrum hardly varies from that in the fresh SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 (Fig. 6g), confirming the solid carbon structure during electrocatalysis.

In summary, we developed symmetrical pyramid arrays on sheet Fe-doped NiO/FeNi3 (SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3) heterostructure based on graphene quantum dots-triggered growth. Spectroscopy analysis reveals that the presence of C-GQDs not only induces the unique configuration but also results in high-valenced Ni δ
+/Fe δ
+ in FeNi3 and Ni(2+
δ
) in NiFeO x , with lower coordination numbers of both Ni and Fe, owing to the larger π-conjugation endowed with strong electron-withdrawing ability compared with BDC ligand. Therefore, such SPA-NiFeO x /FeNi3 heterostructure exhibits superior electrochemical performance toward valorization of PET plastics, with a formic acid selectivity of 91.5% and a Faradaic efficiency of 92%. In-situ spectroscopic studies and DFT calculations reveal the favorable adsorption of EG molecules on Ni δ
+ for cascade dehydrogenation, which is promoted by the enrichment of OH− on nearby Ni(2+
δ
) sites to form glyoxal as intermediate and converted into adsorptive FA that is transferred to Fe δ
+ site for repulsive desorption. This work provides a definite idea for catalyst design toward specific reactions more than the valorization of wastes into value-added chemicals.

Bo Peng: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Sheng Qian: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Shuhui Ma: Validation, Formal analysis. Yi Zhang: Visualization, Data curation. Huaiguo Xue: Supervision. Tengfei Jiang: Software, Funding acquisition. Jingqi Tian: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22102140, the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Grant No. BK20211602), the Qing Lan Project of Yangzhou University, and the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (Yangzhou University, Grant No. SJCX23_1911).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jechem.2025.05.015.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
